coredata.dc:title: Luminescence and magnetic properties of bifunctional nanoparticles composited by nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and gadolinium
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Chinese Society of Rare Earths. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Chinese Society of Rare Earths. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Wanli
Zhou, Hao
Ou, Meigui
Sun, Donghao
Yang, Chunlin
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were combined with gadolinium ions (Gd3+) by a surface modification to obtain magneto-optical dual-functional N-GQDs/Gd3+ nanoparticles. The morphology of obtained composite was characterized by field emission scanning electron microscopy and transmission electron microscopy. Luminescence and magnetic properties were measured by a fluorescence spectrophotometer and a vibrating sample magnetometer, respectively. Results indicate that well-dispersed spherical N-GQDs/Gd3+ nanoparticles have an average diameter of 7 nm. N-doping significantly increases the luminescence of particles with an optimal luminescence intensity at 20 °C and pH = 9. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy results indicate that the N-doping introduces pyrrolic N as an electron donor, enhancing fluorescence by increasing the surface electron cloud density of N-GQDs. In addition, density functional theory calculation results reveal that N-doping reduces the band gap of N-GQDs/Gd3+, enabling electronic transitions to higher energy levels and generating more activation sites, thereby enhancing luminescence. Compared to N-GQDs/Gd3+ prepared at 20 °C, the saturated magnetization of particles prepared at 40 °C is 0.85 emu/g, indicating a better magnetic response. The above results suggest that bifunctional nanomaterials N-GQDs/Gd3+ with excellent optical properties and magnetism can be better used for fluorescence and magnetic resonance imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
Gadolinium
Luminescence
Magnetism
Rare earths

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of GQDs/Gd3+ (a) and N-GQDs/Gd3+ (b); TEM images (c, d), EDS spectrum (e), and EDS elemental mapping images (f–h) of N-GQDs/Gd3+.
FTIR spectra of GQDs, GQDs/Gd3+, and N-GQDs/Gd3+.
(a) XPS full spectrum; C 1s (b), Gd 4d (c) spectra of GQDs/Gd3+ and N-GQDs/Gd3+; (d) N 1s spectra of N-GQDs/Gd3+.
Fluorescence spectra of GQDs, N-GQDs, GQDs/Gd3+, N-GQDs/Gd3+ (a), N-GQDs/Gd3+ nanoparticles in different pH solutions (b), and N-GQDs/Gd3+ nanoparticles at different preparation temperatures (c).
SEM images (a, b) and particle size distribution histogram (c, d) of N-GQDs/Gd3+ prepared at 20 and 40 °C, respectively.
Energy level diagrams of GQDs (a), N-GQDs (b), and N-GQDs/Gd3+ (c).
Zeta potential of N-GQDs/Gd3+ (a) and GQDs/Gd3+ (b) in aqueous solution.

r 1 (a) and r 2 (b) of GQDs/Gd3+ at different Gd3+ concentrations; T 1-weighted (c) and T 2-weighted (d) images of GQDs/Gd3+ at different Gd3+ concentrations; (e) Evolution of magnetization of N-GQDs/Gd3+ with applied magnetic field under the condition of T = 300 K.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: Foundation item: Project supported by Science and Technology Project from Guizhou Province ([2022]031, [2023]267, [2023]016), and Central Government Guides Local Science and Technology Development ([2019]4011).

head.ce:title: Luminescence and magnetic properties of bifunctional nanoparticles composited by nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and gadolinium

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials and Metallurgy, University of Guizhou, Guiyang, 550025, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials and Metallurgy, University of Guizhou, Guiyang 550025, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Local Co-construction Engineering Laboratory for High Performance Metal Structure Material and Manufacture Technology, Guiyang, 550025, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Local Co-construction Engineering Laboratory for High Performance Metal Structure Material and Manufacture Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Local Co-construction Engineering Laboratory for High Performance Metal Structure Material and Manufacture Technology, Guiyang 550025, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering, Guiyang University, Guiyang, 550003, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering, Guiyang University, Guiyang, 550003, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Materials and Metallurgy, University of Guizhou, Guiyang, 550025, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were combined with gadolinium ions (Gd3+) by a surface modification to obtain magneto-optical dual-functional N-GQDs/Gd3+ nanoparticles. The morphology of obtained composite was characterized by field emission scanning electron microscopy and transmission electron microscopy. Luminescence and magnetic properties were measured by a fluorescence spectrophotometer and a vibrating sample magnetometer, respectively. Results indicate that well-dispersed spherical N-GQDs/Gd3+ nanoparticles have an average diameter of 7 nm. N-doping significantly increases the luminescence of particles with an optimal luminescence intensity at 20 °C and pH = 9. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy results indicate that the N-doping introduces pyrrolic N as an electron donor, enhancing fluorescence by increasing the surface electron cloud density of N-GQDs. In addition, density functional theory calculation results reveal that N-doping reduces the band gap of N-GQDs/Gd3+, enabling electronic transitions to higher energy levels and generating more activation sites, thereby enhancing luminescence. Compared to N-GQDs/Gd3+ prepared at 20 °C, the saturated magnetization of particles prepared at 40 °C is 0.85 emu/g, indicating a better magnetic response. The above results suggest that bifunctional nanomaterials N-GQDs/Gd3+ with excellent optical properties and magnetism can be better used for fluorescence and magnetic resonance imaging.

N-doping improves the luminescence intensity of GQDs, and the groups on the surface of N-GQDs are conducive to binding with small biological molecules and introducing the magnetic properties of Gd. The dual-functional N-GQDs/Gd3+ nanoparticles can be used for fluorescence imaging and MRI detection.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para:
Unique physicochemical properties of rare earth elements enable them to have a wide range of applications in biological and medical fields. 1
,
2 Among rare earth elements, Gd has a large electron spin magnetic moment 3 ; therefore, it can enhance contrast agents' magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) signal intensity by reducing the relaxation time of T 1-weighted images. 4 However, free Gd3+ ions inhibit calcium ion channels in the human body and alter intracellular reactive oxygen species balance, leading to parenchymal organ toxicity. 5 Chelates have been developed to encapsulate Gd3+ to mitigate their toxicity. Free Gd3+ release is minimized by macromolecular ligands and Gd3+ chelation, such as Gd-DTPA, the widely used contrast agents in clinical practice. 6
,
7 However, the relaxation performance of these chelates needs further improvement. Moreover, due to early diagnosis and precise treatment requirements for several diseases such as cancer, single diagnosis and treatment technologies such as MRI, fluorescence imaging, and photoacoustic imaging cannot meet the needs. Multimodal imaging is an efficient technology to improve early diagnosis and precise treatment, and multifunctional nanoparticles in MRI and fluorescence can meet the multimodal imaging requirement; therefore, designing multifunctional nanoparticles has become a research hot spot in the biomedical field. 8

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are widely used in biological imaging, 9 drug delivery, 10 sensors, 11 photovoltaic devices, 12 and catalysis 13 due to their good water solubility and biocompatibility, non-toxicity, and strong fluorescence properties. 14–16 In addition, due to their tunable bandgap, charge carrier selectivity, and excellent fluorescence characteristics, GQDs have gained tremendous attention in biomedical research. 17–21 Functional groups at GQDs surface become modifiers for hydrophobic magnetic nanoparticles. 22 Moreover, the better water solubility of small-sized GQDs, compared with long-chain organic surfactants or silica layers with high density, improves the MR property of magnetic nanoparticles after surface modification. 23
,
24 Therefore, GQDs with fluorescent properties can be combined with magnetic nanoparticles to form bifunctional nanocomposites for MR and cell labeling. Li et al. prepared Gd3+ loaded polyethylene glycol-modified graphene oxide quantum dots (GPG), and r 1 values of GPGs significantly increased by 20–30 folds agents over a wide range of static magnetic field strengths, compared to commercially available contrast. Furthermore, folic-acid-modified GPG was suitable for MRI-fluorescence dual-modal tumor targeting imaging in animals with more than 98.3% specific cellular update rate. 25 These research reports suggest that multifunctional nanocarriers can be used as targeted drug carriers for diagnosing various cancers and image-guided chemotherapy.

Heteroatom dopants such as nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), boron (B), and phosphorus (P) in GQDs are effective in further improving the luminescence performance of GQDs composites. 26
,
27 It has been reported that N-doping in GQDs can change their physical and chemical properties and significantly adjust their photoluminescence. 28–30 Compared with undoped GQDs, N-doped GQDs have high quantum yield (QY) and tunable emission wavelengths since N-doping can enhance active sites on GQDs and improve their photoluminescence performance. 31
,
32 Hao et al. mixed different concentrations of urea solution with GQDs solution to achieve the doping of N atoms by hydrothermal method and obtained results indicated that the higher content of Pyrrolic N improved the photoluminescence intensity. Moreover, its PL quantum yield also significantly increased from 9% to 24%. 33 In addition, N-doped GQDs have a high two-photon absorption cross-section, which improves the photomagnetic properties of multifunctional composites and is suitable for deep tissue fluorescence imaging. 34
,
35

Therefore, in this paper, N-doped GQDs with enhanced fluorescence were prepared by a simple hydrothermal method and then surface modified with Gd3+ to obtain the magneto-optical bifunctional nanoparticles for multimodal imaging, diagnosis, and treatment. 36 The morphology, size, colloidal stability, fluorescence, and magnetism of GQDs/Gd3+ and N-GQDs/Gd3+ were investigated. The fluorescence enhancement mechanism was elaborated via density functional theory (DFT) calculation based on first principles. The results provide theoretical and experimental evidence for applying N-GQDs/Gd3+ nanoparticles in biological imaging, medical targeting, disease diagnosis, and treatment.
Citric acid (AR = 99.5%), gadolinium chloride hexahydrate (GdCl3·6H2O, 99.9%), NaOH (AR≥96%), and ammonia (NH3·H2O, 28%–30%) were purchased from Aladdin.

0.5 g citric acid was added to a round bottom flask and heated at 200 °C in an oil bath. When the solid citric acid changed to an orange-red colloid, the round bottom flask was removed, and 10 mol/L sodium hydroxide solution was added and stirred for 10 min until fully dissolved. The orange solution was filtered with 0.22 μm microporous membrane and dialyzed through a 3500 Da dialysis bag for 3 d. The deionized water was changed every 8 h until the pH reached 7. The resulting solution was freeze-dried to a solid powder for subsequent experiments.

10 mL (0.8 mg/mL) GQDs solution and 10 mL (0.8 mmol/L) GdCl3 solution were mixed and thoroughly stirred; the Gd content was 11.5%. After standing for 1 h, the obtained solution was dialyzed through a 3500 Da dialysis bag for 3 d to remove free Gd3+, and the resulting solution was freeze-dried to solid powder for subsequent experiments.

2 g citric acid was mixed with 0.3 mL ammonia solution, heated at 200 °C for 6 h in a high-pressure reactor, and then cooled to room temperature. The resulting dark brown mixture was dissolved in deionized water, and 10 mol/L NaOH aqueous solution was added to adjust pH = 7. The faint yellow solution was magnetically stirred for 30 min and centrifuged at 12000 r/min for 10 min to remove large particles, followed by the dialysis with a 3000 Da dialysis bag for 2 d. The resulting N-GQDs nanoparticles were freeze-dried for 48 h, and the final yellow solid product was ground to powder.
The synthesis procedure was as same as the preparation of GQDs/Gd3+ (mentioned above in 2.2), except that 8 mg N-GQDs replaced the GQDs solid powder.

The morphology of nanocomposites was characterized by field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, SUPRA 40, Zeiss, Germany) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20, USA). The zeta potential and size were obtained by a Malvern laser particle size analyzer (Zetasizer Nano ZS 90, England). Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FTIR, Nicolet iS50, Thermo Fisher, USA) was used to detect the presence of functional groups with a resolution of 4 cm−1 in the range of 500–4000 cm−1 using the KBr pellet technique. The surface chemical composition and bonding states were analyzed by an X-ray photoelectron spectrophotometer (XPS, Escalab 250Xi, Thermo Fisher, USA) equipped with an Al Kα X-ray source (1486.6 eV). The photoluminescent excitation and emission spectra were recorded using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse, Agilent, USA) with a 150 W Xenon lamp at a resolution of 4 nm and a slit width of 5 nm. T-weighted images and relaxation rates of nanoparticles were measured using a 4.7 T MRI scanner (Biospec 4.7/30, Bruker, Germany). The vibrating sample magnetometer (SQUID-VSM) was used to detect the magnetism of samples under an applied magnetic field.

Fig. 1 shows the morphology of prepared GQDs/Gd3+ and N-GQDs/Gd3+ nanoparticles. Fig. 1(a) depicts the spherical shape of GQDs/Gd3+, and the particles are relatively well-dispersed. Fig. 1(b) indicates that N-GQDs/Gd3+ is also spherical and well-dispersed. TEM images of N-GQDs/Gd3+ indicate that the nanoparticles are spherical and about 7 nm in size (Fig. 1(c)). The HRTEM image in the inset of Fig. 1(d) shows that the crystal plane spacing of N-GQDs/Gd3+ is approximately 0.24 nm, corresponding to the (1120) crystal plane spacing of graphene. 37
Fig. 1(e–h) show the EDS pattern and elemental distribution of N-GQDs/Gd3+. In addition to C, O, and N originating from N-GQDs (Fig. 1(e–g)), the Gd element is also observed in Fig. 1(e, h).

Fig. 2 depicts the FTIR spectra of GQDs, GQDs/Gd3+, and N-GQDs/Gd3+. O–H peak at 3443 cm−1 is due to water absorption of samples, and peaks at 1646 and 1402 cm−1 can be attributed to the tensile vibration of CC and C–O, respectively. Compared with GQDs and GQDs/Gd3+, the peaks at 1568 cm−1 in N-GQDs/Gd3+ can be assigned to N–H tensile vibration, indicating the successful doping of N atoms. The strong peak at 647 cm−1 is attributed to the C–H vibrations, and the absence of a Gd peak may be due to its lower contents in the material. 38

Fig. 3 presents the XPS of GQDs/Gd3+ and N-GQDs/Gd3+. As illustrated in the full spectrum, obvious Gd 4d, C 1s, and O 1s peaks are observed at 142, 285, and 531 eV, respectively (Fig. 3(a)). The significant N 1s peak at 400 eV for N-GQDs/Gd3+ suggests that N atoms are successfully doped in the nanocomposites. The high-resolution C 1s peak indicates that CC, C–O, and CO bonds appear in GQDs/Gd3+ and N-GQDs/Gd3+ (Fig. 3(b)). In addition, there are C–N bonds in N-GQDs/Gd3+, indicating the C atoms’ interaction with N atoms. The high-resolution Gd 4d peak in Fig. 3(c) shows the presence of Gd 4d5/2 and Gd 4d3/2 in GQDs/Gd3+ and N-GQDs/Gd3+, validating the presence of Gd3+ in Gd@GQDs. 39 The high-resolution N 1s peaks indicate the presence of pyridinic N (399.1 eV), pyrrolic N (399.9 eV), and graphitic N (400.6 eV) in N-GQDs/Gd3+ (Fig. 3(d)). As an electron donor, pyrrolic N greatly increases the surface electron cloud density of N-GQDs and thus increases the fluorescence. 40 The above results suggest that N atoms are successfully doped in GQDs/Gd3+, and abundant functional groups are consistent with FTIR results.

Fig. 4 (a) indicates the fluorescence patterns of GQDs (λ ex = 360 nm), N-GQDs (λ ex = 340 nm), GQDs/Gd3+ (λ ex = 330 nm), and N-GQDs/Gd3+ (λ ex = 340 nm) at optimal excitation wavelengths. N-GQDs exhibit maximum fluorescence intensity, followed by N-GQDs/Gd3+, GQDs, and GQDs/Gd3+. N-doping improves the luminescence intensity of GQDs and GQDs/Gd3+ due to the formation of N-doping defects during the preparation of N-GQDs. N atoms also occupy the original C atoms in GQDs and then detach from this position to form N vacancies, causing fluorescence under visible light excitation and significantly increasing fluorescence intensity. 41
,
42 Moreover, N-doping causes a blue shift in N-GQDs and N-GQDs/Gd3+, relative to GQDs and GQDs/Gd3+, due to the negative induction effect of N atoms. 43 Compared with N-GQDs, the decrease in fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs/Gd3+ may be attributed to the decrease in GQDs content.

The effects of temperature and pH on N-GQDs/Gd3+ fluorescence were further explored. Fig. 4(b) shows the fluorescence spectra of N-GQDs/Gd3+ nanoparticles at different pH. When the pH of the N-GQDs/Gd3+ solution increases from 2 to 13, the fluorescence intensity increases and then decreases. Under acidic conditions, the z-shaped free sites in GQDs are easily protonated, and the emission triplet state is broken, thus, decreasing the fluorescence. Under alkaline conditions, the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs/Gd3+ is irregular, possibly due to the formation of a small amount of Gd(OH)3 complex. Both H+ and OH– affect surface groups in N-GQDs/Gd3+, causing variations in fluorescence. 44
,
45

Fig. 4(c) depicts the fluorescence spectra of N-GQDs/Gd3+ nanoparticles at different preparation temperatures. The fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs/Gd3+ prepared at 20–40 °C decreases with the temperature increase, probably related to the difference in particle size of N-GQDs/Gd3+ prepared at different temperatures. Fig. 5 shows SEM images and particle size distribution of N-GQDs/Gd3+ prepared at 20 and 40 °C. Fig. 5(a, c) indicate that the particle size of nanoparticles prepared at 20 °C is between 3 and 12 nm, with an average size of 5 nm. The particle size distribution is uniform, which is conducive to fluorescence intensity enhancement of nanoparticles. 46 The particle size of N-GQDs/Gd3+ nanoparticles prepared at 40 °C is within 4–18 nm, with an average particle size of 7 nm, as shown in Fig. 5(b, d). Compared to 20 °C, the particle size of nanoparticles prepared at 40 °C is slightly larger, and the fluorescence intensity decreases slightly due to the nanoparticles' aggregation.

To illustrate the fluorescence enhancement mechanism of N-doping GQDs/Gd3+, the energy bands of GQDs, N-GQDs, and N-GQDs/Gd3+ were calculated by DFT calculations, and results are illustrated in Fig. 6 . The band gaps of GQDs and N-GQDs are 2.706 and 0.224 eV, respectively (Fig. 6(a, b)), indicating that N-doping reduces the band gap of GQDs. The smaller band gap benefits the electrons' transition to higher energy levels, forming more activation sites and thus enhancing the luminescence of GQDs. 47 When the N-GQDs are combined with Gd3+, the band gap of N-GQDs/Gd3+ is 0.501 eV (Fig. 6(c)). Compared to N-GQDs, the band gap of N-GQDs/Gd3+ increases, making the electron transition more difficult and decreasing fluorescence.

Fig. 7 presents the Zeta potential of N-GQDs/Gd3+ and GQDs/Gd3+ in aqueous solutions, which is −43.43 mV (Fig. 7(a)) and −23.79 mV (Fig. 7(b)), respectively. Compared to GQDs/Gd3+, the Zeta potential of N-GQDs/Gd3+ nanoparticles increases by 1.8 folds since N-doping introduces pyridine and tetravalent N into the C backbone of GQDs, and creates rich marginal functional groups on N-GQDs layer, which improves N-GQDs/Gd3+ water solubility, thus improving the colloidal stability and biocompatibility of nanocomposites in water. 48
,
49 These results indicate that N-GQDs/Gd3+ nanoparticles can meet the stability requirement for biological applications and maintain long-term circulation in-vivo applications.

Fig. 8 (a, b) display the evolution of longitudinal relaxation rate r 1 (1/T 1) and lateral relaxation rate r 2 (1/T 2) of GQDs/Gd3+ with Gd3+ concentration, respectively. r 1 and r 2 are both linearly positively correlated with the Gd3+ concentration, and the r 1 and r 2 values are 18.02 (mmol/L)−1/s (Fig. 8(a)), and 22.09 (mmol/L)−1/s (Fig. 8(b)), respectively. Moreover, the longitudinal relaxation is 4 times that of commercial contrast agent Gd-DTPA (r 1 = 4.5 (mmol/L)−1/s). Since r 2/r 1 = 1.22 < 10, using GQDs/Gd3+ nanocomposites as a qualified T 1 contrast agent is conducive. Fig. 8(c, d) show T 1-and T 2-weighted images of GQDs/Gd3+ at different Gd3+ concentrations. When the Gd3+ concentration increases, the quantitative signals improve; the T 1-weighted images get brighter and brighter, while the T 2-weighted images get darker and darker.

Temperature is important among many influencing factors in the optical and magnetic properties of N-GQDs/Gd3+ nanoparticles. Therefore, the magnetizations of the N-GQDs/Gd3+ synthesized at different temperatures are investigated, and results are illustrated in Fig. 8(e). The saturated magnetization values of samples prepared at 20 and 40 °C are 0.72 and 0.85 emu/g, respectively, suggesting that the nanocomposites are paramagnetic with good magnetic responsiveness at both temperatures. As shown in Fig. 5(a, b), the size of nanoparticles prepared at 20 °C is smaller than those prepared at 40 °C, probably causing more surface defects to weaken the magnetic reactions, resulting in a decrease in saturation magnetization. 50 Therefore, the corresponding magnetization is relatively enhanced at 40 °C.

In this work, spherical and well-dispersed N-GQDs/Gd3+ nanoparticles were successfully prepared. N-doping reduces the band gap and provides more active sites, benefitting the electronic transitions in GQDs, and improving the luminescence of N-GQDs/Gd3+ nanocomposites. The optimal luminescence intensity of N-GQDs/Gd3+ nanoparticles is observed at pH = 9 and 20 °C. The longitudinal relaxation rate r 1 of GQDs/Gd3+ is 18.02 (mmol/L)−1/s, about 4 times of commercial contrast agent Gd-DTPA (4.5 (mmol/L)−1/s). The saturation magnetization of N-GQDs/Gd3+ prepared at different temperatures (20 and 40 °C) is 0.72 and 0.83 emu/g, respectively, indicating a good magnetic response. In addition, the Zeta potential of N-GQDs/Gd3+ is −43.43 mV, 1.8 times that of GQDs/Gd3+ (−23.79 mV), displaying high colloidal stability and biocompatibility in water. These results suggest that N-GQDs/Gd3+ nanoparticles with excellent dual-functional properties have important potential applications in biological imaging, drug targeting, and in vivo diagnostics.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.
